%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 3.2 Useful Signal Operations

> **Section Objective:** Learn how discrete-time signals shift when the index inside x[ ] changes.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- discrete-time shifting
- delay / right shift
- advance / left shift
- integer shift M

## 1. A Delay Is a Right Shift

A discrete-time signal \(x[n]\) is a sequence of samples placed at integer \(n\)-values on the horizontal axis. Each sample has a specific index location.

When you **delay** the sequence by 5 units, every sample appears 5 index positions later — the entire graph slides to the right.

**Minimal example:** The sample originally at \(n = 0\) appears at \(n = 5\) after a delay of 5.

### KEY INSIGHT

Delay = later on the \(n\)-axis = rightward movement. The word "delay" and the phrase "right shift" describe the same operation.

## 2. The general shift rule

**Symbol definitions:**
- \(x[n]\) — the original sequence, with samples at integer indices
- \(x_s[n]\) — the shifted (delayed) sequence
- \(n\) — the integer sample index
- \(5\) — the delay amount, in samples

**When to use it:** When a problem says "delay by 5" or "right-shift by 5", write \(x_s[n] = x[n - 5]\).

**Exam trigger:** A positive delay appears as \(n - 5\) inside the brackets — the subtraction of a positive number.

**Common misuse:** Students see the minus sign and assume left shift. This is wrong. \(x[n - 5]\) shifts the sequence to the **right** by 5 positions.

$$x_s[n] = x[n - 5]$$

## 3. The sign trap

The same idea works for any integer shift \(M\). The shift amount \(M\) must be an integer because discrete-time samples live only at integer index locations — there is no sample between \(n = 2\) and \(n = 3\).

**Reading the sign:**
- \(x[n - 3]\) — delay by 3 (right shift by 3)
- \(x[n + 2]\) — advance by 2 (left shift by 2)

### KEY INSIGHT

Do not decide direction from the visible \(+\) or \(-\) sign alone. Always match the bracket expression to the template \(n - M\) first, then read off \(M\) and its sign.

$$x_s[n] = x[n - M]$$
*This is the core rule for discrete-time shifting.

**Symbol definitions:**
- \(M\) — integer shift amount
- \(x[n - M]\) — the shifted sequence

**Direction rules:**
- If \(M > 0\): right shift (delay) by \(M\) samples
- If \(M < 0\): the expression becomes \(x[n + |M|]\), a left shift (advance) by \(|M|\) samples
- If \(M = 0\): no shift

**When to use it:** Whenever a sequence is shifted along the \(n\)-axis.

**Exam trigger:** Identify the expression inside the brackets first. Rewrite it in the form \(n - M\), then read the sign of \(M\).

**Common misuse:** Deciding direction from the visible \(+\) or \(-\) sign instead of matching the full expression to \(n - M\). Always substitute and simplify before concluding.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. The Sign Trap

The most common error is reading the visible \(+\) or \(-\) sign and immediately deciding direction. This fails.

**The correct method:** Rewrite the argument as \(n - M\), then read \(M\).

**Near-miss example:** \(x[n + 5]\) looks like a right shift because of the \(+\). But rewriting gives \(x[n - (-5)]\), so \(M = -5\). The signal **advances left by 5**, not right.

### EXAM TIP

First match the bracket to \(n - M\), then decide whether \(M\) is positive or negative.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- \(x_s[n] = x[n - 5]\) delays the sequence by 5 samples, moving every sample 5 positions to the right.
- \(x_s[n] = x[n - M]\) is the general shift rule; \(M\) must be an integer.
- \(M > 0\) means delay / right shift: samples appear later on the \(n\)-axis.
- \(M < 0\) means advance / left shift: rewrite \(x[n - M]\) as \(x[n + |M|]\) to confirm direction.

*Next, the same signal-operation mindset extends to other transformations of discrete-time signals.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
